evidence_quotes: Personalised advertisements will always lead to making data collection a core metric for advertisers.
Or that you have to buy some tokens with money to obfuscate the real cost of things, or that some online offerings only let you, for example, add a balance of the equivalent of 10 onto an account.
"20% discount" from the outset to prey on people by creating FOMO (fear of missing out).
change the terms of sale after the sale.
"We can take away your product whenever we feel like it."

paragraphs.text: Good evening, targeted advertising itself addresses most of the core points of the Digital Fairness Act. Personalised advertisements will always lead to making data collection a core metric for advertisers. And that also means that data on when I use my toilet, what kind of medication I was looking up online, or other private information becomes valuable to these groups and people. This in itself is a huge problem that should be examined more closely by the government. Violating people's privacy is too attractive and lucrative as long as targeted advertising is a thing. And the industry will never regulate itself in a way that is respectful or beneficial for the people if it means a loss in revenue. We have seen it all too often, and there has to be a change through new policies that combat the endless greed in this sector. The entertainment sector, especially in the digital realm, is becoming an increasingly significant issue when it comes to consumer rights. It is far too common for companies like Mojang to change the terms of sale after the sale. Or movies that got initially bought by clicking a big, fat "Buy Now" button suddenly are unavailable because the platform decided to take them offline. It is also a big trend that items online and even in video games get released and suddenly have a "20% discount" from the outset to prey on people by creating FOMO (fear of missing out). Or that you have to buy some tokens with money to obfuscate the real cost of things, or that some online offerings only let you, for example, add a balance of the equivalent of 10 onto an account. The sold goods (which often are just limited licenses that get falsely advertised as a sale of a good that gets falsely advertised as something you are allowed to keep) will frequently be priced in a way that you have some leftover balance. This is designed to incentivise spending so the "leftover balance" is not put to waste. All of this comes from the perspective of a 28-year-old person who grew up with software that you just bought and owned. And software that was already tested and not some unfinished product that gets licensed to you under false premises. And speaking personally, I'm so done with being treated like a lesser being by these big corporations. And we need a lot more consumer rights in this and other spaces to combat this kind of abusive behaviour. There is also a lack of enforcement of current rules. Especially when it comes to parts of EULAs that state bogus reasoning along the lines of, "We can take away your product whenever we feel like it." Stuff like that cannot be legal as far as I know. And we also need rules that bigger companies or other entities won't see as "added cost" that they can calculate into their behaviour, or have the end user pay by raising prices. I am well aware that I am asking for a lot here, but this is dear to my heart and important to me. I hope that some aspects of my feedback can be implemented, considered, or discussed. Thank you for taking the time to read this rather personal feedback, and I am looking forward to what comes next.
